children.name: Hyla
children.description: Small green tree frogs with adhesive toe pads enabling climbing on smooth surfaces. The European Tree Frog is Sweden's rarest amphibian, persisting only in a handful of sites in southernmost Skåne, where it clings to reed stems and calls loudly from pond margins on warm evenings.

children.name: Hyla arborea
children.description: Sweden's rarest amphibian; brilliant green with dark lateral stripe; adhesive toe pads; calls from May to July; IUCN Least Concern but severely declining in northern range.

children.name: Hyla japonica
children.description: Widespread in East Asia; changes colour between green and brown depending on temperature.

children.name: Hyla cinerea
children.description: A slender bright-green frog of the southeastern United States, often with a pale lateral stripe. Abundant around ponds and swamps; the state amphibian of Georgia and Louisiana. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Hyla versicolor
children.description: A tetraploid (four sets of chromosomes) species of eastern North America that can change colour from gray to green for camouflage. Tolerates freezing by producing glycerol as a natural antifreeze.

children.name: Hyla meridionalis
children.description: A green tree frog of the western Mediterranean lacking the dark flank stripe of H. arborea. Found in Iberia, southern France, and northwest Africa. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Hyla intermedia
children.description: An Italian endemic tree frog formerly considered part of H. arborea. Inhabits wetlands, rice paddies, and pond margins across the Italian peninsula. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Pseudacris
children.description: Chorus frogs of North America.

children.name: Pseudacris crucifer
children.description: The defining sound of North American spring; only 3 cm long; X-shaped marking on back; chorus audible a kilometre away.

children.name: Pseudacris regilla
children.description: Most frequently heard frog in western North America; Hollywood soundtrack standard.

children.name: Pseudacris triseriata
children.description: A small chorus frog of central North America marked with three dark dorsal stripes. Its rising trill, like running a finger over a comb, is among the first amphibian sounds of spring.

children.name: Pseudacris cadaverina
children.description: A cryptic gray-brown frog of rocky streams in southern California and Baja. Its mottled skin matches granite boulders almost perfectly, making it nearly invisible when still.

children.name: Agalychnis
children.description: Spectacular Neotropical tree frogs.

children.name: Agalychnis callidryas
children.description: The most photographed frog in the world; vivid red eyes, orange feet, and blue flanks; nocturnal; lays eggs on leaves overhanging water — tadpoles drop in on hatching.

children.name: Agalychnis moreletii
children.description: A Central American leaf frog with red eyes and orange flanks, distinguished from the red-eyed tree frog by its darker body. Critically Endangered, hit hard by chytrid fungus and habitat loss.

children.name: Agalychnis spurrelli
children.description: A leaf frog of Central America and northwest South America able to parachute between trees using extensively webbed feet. Breeds in explosive mass aggregations over forest pools.

children.name: Litoria
children.description: Diverse Australasian tree frogs.

children.name: Litoria caerulea
children.description: The iconic dumpy tree frog; calm temperament; deposits lipid on skin to reduce water loss; enormously popular in captivity.

children.name: Litoria infrafrenata
children.description: World's largest tree frog at up to 14 cm; loud barking call.

children.name: Litoria aurea
children.description: A striking green-and-gold frog of southeastern Australia, once abundant but now Vulnerable after dramatic declines from chytrid fungus and habitat loss. Unusually active by day and basks in the sun.

children.name: Litoria ewingii
children.description: A small adaptable tree frog of southeastern Australia and Tasmania, also introduced to New Zealand. Its long whirring trill is a common night sound around gardens and wetlands.

children.name: Litoria gracilenta
children.description: A slender bright-green frog with yellow on the thighs and feet, found along the eastern Australian coast. Frequently encountered in gardens and bananas, earning the nickname 'graceful tree frog'.

children.name: Dendropsophus
children.description: Small Neotropical tree frogs, formerly part of Hyla, characterised by their tiny size and yellow-spotted hidden surfaces.

children.name: Dendropsophus ebraccatus
children.description: A tiny Central and South American frog notable for flexible egg-laying, depositing eggs on leaves or directly in water depending on predation risk and conditions. A model species for the study of phenotypic plasticity.

children.name: Dendropsophus minutus
children.description: One of the most widespread South American frogs, a tiny tan species heard chirping around ponds and ditches across the continent. Likely a complex of several cryptic species.

children.name: Phyllomedusa
children.description: Leaf frogs of South America that walk slowly along branches rather than hopping, and whose skin secretions have been studied for medical peptides.

children.name: Phyllomedusa bicolor
children.description: A large green Amazonian leaf frog whose skin secretion ('kambô') is used in Indigenous ritual and contains bioactive peptides studied by pharmacologists. Walks deliberately, hand over hand, through the canopy.

children.name: Phyllomedusa sauvagii
children.description: A frog of the dry Gran Chaco that secretes a waxy lipid coating which it wipes over its body to prevent water loss, allowing it to survive in arid scrub. Excretes uric acid like a reptile to conserve water.

children.name: Smilisca
children.description: Mexican and Central American tree frogs of lowland forest and seasonal pools.

children.name: Smilisca baudinii
children.description: A large, blotchy brown-and-green tree frog ranging from southern Texas through Central America. Aestivates buried in mud during the dry season and emerges to breed explosively with the first rains.

children.name: Scinax
children.description: A speciose genus of small, agile snouted tree frogs across the Neotropics.

children.name: Scinax ruber
children.description: A widespread, highly adaptable tree frog of South America and the Caribbean, often colonising disturbed and urban habitats. Recognised by reddish thighs flashed when it leaps.

children.name: Scinax boulengeri
children.description: A Central American tree frog with a sharply pointed snout and fleshy projections on the heels. Named for the prolific herpetologist George Albert Boulenger.
